steps.title: Determine what the task requires
steps.content:
The goal is to "explain how soap removes a surface contaminant" — meaning the answer must describe the step-by-step mechanism, not just properties of soap or contaminants.
  • Choice A — Surface contaminants like dirt and oils are hydrophobic, not hydrophilic—meaning they repel water rather than attract it.: Describes only a property of contaminants — gives no steps showing removal. ✗
  • Choice B — Micelles are circular structures created when soap molecules attach themselves to dirt or oils.: Describes one step (micelle formation) but omits how the contaminant is actually lifted off the surface. ✗
  • Choice C — To remove dirt from a surface, soap molecules form a circular structure, or micelle, around the contaminant; next, the molecules encase the micelle in water, lifting it—and the dirt—off the surface.: Traces the full removal mechanism from micelle formation through the final lift-off. ✓
  • Choice D — Dirt, an example of a hydrophobic surface contaminant, can be removed from a surface by soap, which forms as the result of a chemical reaction between fats and either sodium or potassium hydroxide.: Explains how soap is manufactured, not how it removes contaminants. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only one choice traces the complete step-by-step process of contaminant removal. That is the correct answer.
